plant.description:
Botanical Family: The provided sources do not specify the botanical family for tea. (Note: General scientific knowledge outside the provided sources places tea in the Theaceae family, derived from the plant Camellia sinensis, which you may want to independently verify).
2. Brief Description and Common Forms
Description: Tea is a widely consumed human beverage known for its stimulant properties. It contains methylxanthine alkaloids—specifically caffeine and theobromine13. While these compounds are safe for human consumption, they are highly toxic to cats and dogs, acting as powerful stimulants that can severely disrupt their central nervous and cardiovascular systems14.
Common Forms: In the household, tea is most commonly encountered as a brewed liquid beverage45. It is also found in its dry forms, such as loose tea leaves or tea bags, and is frequently included as a base ingredient in various commercial energy drinks and iced beverages1....

toxins.name: Caffeine
toxins.description:
in cats: Caffeine acts through three primary molecular mechanisms. First, it causes competitive antagonism (blocking) of cellular adenosine receptors (A
1
	​

 and A
2
	​

), which normally inhibit the central nervous system (CNS) and heart; blocking them leads to severe CNS stimulation, constriction of blood vessels, diuresis, and a rapid heart rate (tachycardia)23. Second, it inhibits the enzyme phosphodiesterase, causing a dangerous accumulation of intracellular cyclic AMP23. Third, it modulates calcium fluxes by increasing the entry of extracellular calcium and inhibiting the sarcoplasmic reticulum from sequestering it, which abnormally amplifies the contractility and strength of skeletal and cardiac muscles23.
Any notes on concentration or potency: The toxic dose of caffeine for cats is 80 to 150 mg per kilogram of body weight4. Cats metabolize caffeine significantly slower than humans, making them highly sensitive to its effects2. While brewed black or green tea poses a hazard, ingesting dry tea leaves or tea bags is considered extremely dangerous because the caffeine is highly concentrated
toxins.concentration_notes: or potency: The toxic dose of caffeine for cats is 80 to 150 mg per kilogram of body weight4. Cats metabolize caffeine significantly slower than humans, making them highly sensitive to its effects2. While brewed black or green tea poses a hazard, ingesting dry tea leaves or tea bags is considered extremely dangerous because the caffeine is highly concentrated

toxins.name: Theobromine
toxins.description:
in cats: Theobromine belongs to the same class of alkaloids as caffeine and shares the exact same toxicological mechanisms. It acts as a powerful stimulant by blocking adenosine receptors, inhibiting phosphodiesterase, and increasing intracellular calcium levels23. This results in overstimulation of the cardiovascular and nervous systems, leading to hyperactivity, tremors, and cardiac arrhythmias23.
Any notes on concentration or potency: While tea is most recognized for its caffeine content, it also contains theobromine2.... The median lethal dose (LD
50
	​

) of theobromine specifically for cats is 200 mg/kg7. Just like caffeine, cats metabolize theobromine very slowly, allowing the chemical to stay in the bloodstream and quickly reach life-threatening levels
toxins.concentration_notes:
or potency: While tea is most recognized for its caffeine content, it also contains theobromine2.... The median lethal dose (LD
50
	​

) of theobromine specifically for cats is 200 mg/kg7. Just like caffeine, cats metabolize theobromine very slowly, allowing the chemical to stay in the bloodstream and quickly reach life-threatening levels

symptoms.name: 1. Gastrointestinal Upset (Vomiting and Diarrhea)
symptoms.onset: 2 to 12 hours (often within 2 to 4 hours as the initial sign of toxicity)
symptoms.notes: These are typically the very first symptoms to manifest after a cat ingests tea (which contains the methylxanthines caffeine and theobromine)1. Early vomiting can actually be beneficial, as it helps remove some of the unabsorbed toxic caffeine from the cat's body

symptoms.name: Increased Thirst and Urination
symptoms.onset: 2 to 12 hours (often follows initial gastrointestinal signs)
symptoms.notes: The caffeine and theobromine in tea act as diuretics, increasing fluid loss and causing the cat to pass large amounts of urine12. This drives excessive thirst and can quickly lead to dehydration

symptoms.name: Severe Neurological Overstimulation
symptoms.onset: 2 to 12 hours (progressing as the toxins are fully absorbed)
symptoms.notes: Methylxanthines block cellular adenosine receptors, leading to severe central nervous system overstimulation56. The cat will become extremely agitated and excited, which can rapidly progress to lack of coordination, severe muscle tremors, grand mal seizures, and potentially a coma

symptoms.name: Cardiovascular Dysfunction
symptoms.notes: The stimulants in tea increase intracellular calcium levels, which abnormally enhances the strength and frequency of cardiac muscle contractions56. This results in very rapid, irregular heartbeats, high blood pressure, and ultimately cardiac arrest or a heart attack in the terminal phase

symptoms.name: Respiratory Distress
symptoms.onset: 6 to 12 hours (often in the mid to terminal phases)
symptoms.notes: As the cat's cardiovascular and central nervous systems become overwhelmed, it will pant and breathe very rapidly2.... In severe poisonings, this progresses to a bluish tinge of the mucous membranes due to poor oxygenation, culminating in complete respiratory failure

symptoms.name: Hyperthermia
symptoms.notes: The extreme overstimulation of the nervous system, combined with continuous hyperactivity and severe muscle tremors, generates excessive internal heat, causing the cat's core body temperature to spike to dangerous levels

treatments.name: Because tea contains the toxic methylxanthine alkaloids caffeine and theobromine, the veterinary response to a cat ingesting tea follows the emergency protocols for methylxanthine toxicosis
Emergency Stabilization (Seizure Control and Cardiovascular Support)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If the cat presents with severe clinical signs, the veterinarian will immediately administer medications such as diazepam or barbiturates to control seizures2. For cardiovascular issues, medications like metoprolol or propranolol are given for rapid heart rates (tachyarrhythmias), or atropine for abnormally slow heart rates (bradycardia), alongside artificial respiration if needed2.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: This is the most urgent priority to stabilize a symptomatic cat. Crucially, lidocaine cannot be used in cats to treat ventricular arrhythmias, as it is contraindicated and toxic to them

treatments.name: Early Gastrointestinal Decontamination
treatments.description:
of the procedure: If the tea was ingested recently (e.g., within 1 hour) and the cat has not yet developed clinical signs, the veterinarian will safely induce vomiting to expel the tea2. For cats, dexmedetomidine is the preferred emetic3.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: If the cat is already seizing or heavily sedated from anticonvulsants, inducing vomiting is dangerous; in these cases, a gastric lavage (stomach pumping) should be considered instead

treatments.name: Toxin Adsorption (Activated Charcoal)
treatments.description:
of the procedure: Following decontamination, the cat is given oral doses of activated charcoal (1–4 g/kg) to bind the remaining caffeine and theobromine in the gastrointestinal tract so it cannot be absorbed2.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Because methylxanthines undergo enterohepatic recirculation (they are processed by the liver, secreted back into the intestines, and reabsorbed), repeated doses of activated charcoal must be administered every 3 hours for up to 72 hours2. Antiemetic medications like metoclopramide can be given to control persistent vomiting so the cat can keep the charcoal down

treatments.name: Fluid Diuresis and Urinary Excretion Support
treatments.description:
of the procedure: The cat is placed on IV fluids to assist in stabilizing cardiovascular function and to hasten the urinary excretion of the methylxanthines2. A urinary catheter is also placed to continuously drain the bladder2.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Placing a urinary catheter is a critical step because methylxanthines and their toxic metabolites can be reabsorbed directly across the bladder wall back into the bloodstream if urine is allowed to sit

treatments.name: Ongoing Symptomatic and Supportive Care
treatments.description:
of the procedure: The veterinary team continuously monitors the cat's cardiac status via electrocardiography (ECG), actively manages the cat's body temperature (thermoregulation), and corrects any acid/base or electrolyte abnormalities that arise2.
Any situation-specific
treatments.notes: Clinical signs of methylxanthine poisoning can persist for up to 72 hours in severe cases2. Medications such as erythromycin and corticosteroids must be strictly avoided during treatment, as they actively interfere with the body's ability to excrete methylxanthines
